title: Order-to-Cash Cycle Time and Bottleneck Analysis
dataSources: Primary: `index=business` with Splunk DB Connect (Splunkbase 2686) fed sourcetype `dbx:erp_orders`, produced by JDBC rising-column inputs—representative stanza `[db://hana/rs_erp_orders]` polling SAP S/4HANA stage histories hourly (`cron_schedule = 0 * * * *`) with watermark column `CHANGEDATE` or `LASTUPDATE` on your curated stage-event projection that unions operational timestamps drawn from SD (`VBAK`, `VBAP`), deliveries (`LIKP`, `LIPS`), billing (`VBRK`, `VBRP`), and FI clearing pointers (`BSAD`). Alternate Oracle JDBC input `[oracle://corp/rs_oracle_o2c]` executes SQL views joining `OE_ORDER_HEADERS_ALL`, `WSH_DELIVERY_DETAILS`, `RA_CUSTOMER_TRX_ALL`, and `AR_PAYMENT_SCHEDULES_ALL` with rising watermark `LAST_UPDATE_DATE`. NetSuite deployments may use SuiteAnalytics Connect ODBC behind DB Connect with watermark `LASTMODIFIEDDATE`. Each indexed row must expose `order_id`, `stage_timestamp`, raw ERP stage (`stage_raw`), mapped canonical stage (`stage_canonical`), optional `sales_org`, `warehouse_id`, `customer_tier`, and `order_value` so stream-ordered bottleneck math can segment B2B versus B2C latencies.
app: Splunk DB Connect (Splunkbase 2686) — package name `splunk_app_db_connect` on Splunkbase; install on Heavy Forwarder or dedicated DB Connect server per Splunk deployment guidance.
spl:
index=business sourcetype="dbx:erp_orders" earliest=-90d@d
| eval stage_canon=coalesce(stage_canonical, stage_std, upper(trim(coalesce(stage_raw,""))), trim(coalesce(stage,"")))
| eval stage_time=coalesce(strptime(stage_timestamp, "%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S"), strptime(stage_timestamp, "%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S.%3Q"), strptime(stage_timestamp, "%Y-%m-%d"))
| where isnotnull(stage_time)
| sort order_id, stage_time
| dedup order_id stage_canon
| streamstats current=f latest(stage_time) as prev_time latest(stage_canon) as prev_stage by order_id
| eval stage_duration_hours=if(isnotnull(prev_time), round((stage_time-prev_time)/3600, 4), null())
| where isnull(stage_duration_hours) OR stage_duration_hours>0
| stats avg(stage_duration_hours) as avg_hours perc95(stage_duration_hours) as p95_hours perc99(stage_duration_hours) as p99_hours count as transitions dc(order_id) as orders_seen by stage_canon
| eval avg_days=round(avg_hours/24, 2)
| sort stage_canon
| table stage_canon avg_hours avg_days p95_hours p99_hours transitions orders_seen
description: Monitors Splunk-ingested ERP stage-transition rows (`dbx:erp_orders`) by reconstructing each sales document timeline with stream-ordered analytics after canonical stage aliasing and duplicate suppression—surfacing where elapsed hours accumulate between Ordered→Confirmed→Allocated→Picked→Packed→Shipped→Delivered→Invoiced→Cash_Applied pairs. Tail-heavy durations separated into ninety-fifth/ninety-ninth percentile bands spotlight Fulfillment labour choke points versus billing backlog versus treasury collection slack without pretending inbound procurement supplier reliability or outbound carrier-only punctuality scores answer the same question—those topics remain sibling inventory, shipment SLA, supplier OTD, perfect-order composite, capacity planning, or reverse-logistics use cases.
value: Chief Operating Officers and VP Supply Chain translate bottleneck histograms into staffing plans, warehouse overtime budgets, and SIOP escalation triggers before quarterly earnings narratives fracture; CFO and treasury peers overlay Splunk-derived median invoice-to-cash dwell against Days Sales Outstanding governance thresholds because lengthening conversion cycles silently inflate working capital despite steady headline revenue. Operations excellence teams gain quantified defense during retros when carriers blame docks—or Finance challenges warehouses—because Splunk anchors timestamps on immutable ERP postings rather than anecdotal ticketing timelines.
implementation: (1) Materialize JDBC rising-column extracts per ERP tier delivering uniform `(order_id, stage_canonical, stage_timestamp)` tuples into `dbx:erp_orders`; (2) publish canonical stage lookup CSV bridging SAP status profiles, Oracle OM lifecycle phases, or NetSuite sales-order statuses; (3) deploy bottleneck SPL plus Dashboard Studio waterfall/sankey companions referencing ninety-day rolling windows; (4) attach Ops routing for Fulfillment regressions versus treasury paging rules for invoice-to-cash anomalies.
visualization: Dashboard Studio bundle: waterfall chart sequencing canonical stage dwell aggregates for median-and-tail overlays; directed sankey (`splunk.sankey` or exported chord-ready aggregates) illustrating volumetric transitions between coarse stages when cardinality manageable; trellised line trends comparing ninety-day rolling median dwell per warehouse versus enterprise SLA corridor; single-value median Days Sales Outstanding tile sourced from paired finance extracts reconciling AR balances (`BSID`/`BSAD`) against billed revenue—not Splunk pretending standalone GAAP closure; supplemental bubble heatmaps faceting enterprise versus SMB customer tiers.

references.title: SAP Help Portal — Sales Document Tables (VBAK/VBAP overview)
Oracle SCM Cloud — Ship Confirm and OM References
Oracle NetSuite Applications Suite documentation (Help Center)
Splunk Lantern — Identifying supply chain issues (process visibility)

knownFalsePositives: Ocean freight lanes elongate Delivered timestamps versus dock Ship timestamps without implying dock inefficiency—carrier variability masks Fulfillment KPI honesty unless segmented by Incoterms. Planned ERP blackouts freeze postings yet inventory physically moves—duration spikes reflect maintenance calendars not Operations regression. SAP finance accelerators replay aged CDHDR pointers during backlog drains near German fiscal-year-end producing duplicate-looking dwell spikes absent genuine rework. Same-second RFID tunnel scans collapse pick versus pack deltas despite hourly labour elapsed—parallel-stage anomalies inflate pick→pack KPI optimism when timestamps collide. Partial shipments duplicate lifecycle tuples without netting residual quantities—Splunk aggregates mis-count throughput intensity unless keyed by delivered quantity tiers. Customer-driven pickup lanes bypass carrier milestones entirely yet analysts mistake skipped legs for systemic latency. Heavy month-end billing batches lengthen invoice lag though fulfilment stayed steady—Finance rhythm dominates invoice→cash variance temporarily. Extended AR collection stretches unrelated to fulfilment excellence appear when treasury grants ninety-day enterprise Net terms deliberately—misroutes Ops accountability without segmentation by payment_term tier. EDI ASN batching delays Delivered scans though product left the building hours earlier—true supply-chain noise confuses last-mile posture but is not dock failure. Freight-broker handoffs create limbo timestamps between Shipped versus Invoiced splits when documentation passes through non-integrated 3PL portals. Credit-hold pause-and-release sequences stretch Confirmed→Allocated gaps while warehouse capacity sits idle—misread as labour shortage absent credit status flags. Multi-currency FX revaluation postings near quarter close disturb naive cash-application math versus functional-currency expectations even when physical delivery performance was sound.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Splunk Enterprise or Splunk Cloud search tier compatible with splunkVersions below; Heavy Forwarder or equivalent DB Connect execution host reachable to JDBC endpoints inside your ERP VLAN or jump-box path.
• Splunk DB Connect (Splunkbase 2686) with identities for SAP HANA (`com.sap.db.jdbc.Driver`, `ngdbc.jar`), Oracle thin JDBC (`oracle.jdbc.OracleDriver`), or NetSuite SuiteAnalytics Connect / replicated warehouse reachable via TLS JDBC—never expose ERP SYSDBA credentials to Splunk roles directly.
• Network approvals for outbound TCP from the DB Connect worker to SAP indexserver (often 3NN15), Oracle SCAN listeners (1521/tcp), or warehouse Synapse/BigQuery ODBC bridges—attach firewall ticket IDs to your change record.
• Finance sign-off that cycle-time KPIs complement—not replace—subledger close controls; Supply Chain agrees on canonical stage labels mapped from SAP SD (`VBAK`-driven confirmations), Oracle OM (`OE_ORDER_HEADERS_ALL` lifecycle states), or NetSuite `salesorder.status` transitions.

**Step 1 — Configure rising-column ingestion from ERP stage histories**

Stand up one DB Connect input per authoritative stage-event projection—generic pattern Stanza names such as `[mysql://corp/rs_erp_orders]` or `[db://hana/rs_o2c_stage_history]` depending on UI export (Splunk merges Identity + Connection + Input into runtime `db_inputs.conf`). Primary SAP pattern: nightly warehouse SQL unions CDHDR/CDPOS change-pointer snapshots with operational extracts so each row carries `(VBELN AS order_id, stage_canonical, ERPDATETIME AS stage_timestamp, VKORG, WERKS, NETWR_DOC_CURR)` alongside provenance columns; HANA JDBC URL `jdbc:sap://<host>:3<NN>15/?databaseName=<SID>` with watermark on `CHANGEDATE` from your custom Z-table or last-changed audit column (some teams expose `LASTCHANGEDDATETIME` from a Data Services layer instead of polling base `VBAK` every run). Poll interval: hourly cron `0 * * * *` is common; set batch size ≤5k rows when HANA Workload Class is shared with report users. Oracle pattern: JDBC to a read-only view joining `OE_ORDER_HEADERS_ALL`, `WSH_DELIVERY_DETAILS`, `RA_CUSTOMER_TRX_ALL`, and `AR_PAYMENT_SCHEDULES_ALL` keyed by `ORDER_NUMBER`/`SOURCE_HEADER_ID`, ordered with an in-database `ROW_NUMBER()` over stage time so Splunk receives one row per transition (CONNECT BY hierarchies remain in SQL, not SPL). NetSuite pattern: SuiteAnalytics Connect ODBC through DB Connect with sourcetype `dbx:netsuite_o2c_row`, watermark `LASTMODIFIEDDATE`. Emit sourcetype `dbx:erp_orders` into `index=business`; retain raw `stage_raw` plus mapped `stage_canonical` via lookup CSV keyed by ERP release.

Props-time alignment: prefer `_TIME_PREFIX = stage_timestamp` mapped with `TIME_FORMAT` spanning `%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S` and ISO-8601 variants so Splunk `_time` reflects operational truth—not indexer arrival—for trending.

**Step 2 — Create bottleneck characterization searches**

Paste the SPL saved beside this UC into Search; clone before altering tokens if you tier dashboards by `sales_org`. The logic sorts each order_id ascending by parsed clock time, collapses accidental duplicate postings per `(order_id, stage_canonical)` pair, then applies stream computing so each row inherits the immediately preceding stage timestamp inside that order (`current=f` prevents the running aggregate from swallowing the active row when deriving deltas). Hours between successive canonical stages yield pair durations sensitive to labour shortages at pick-pack, carrier slack ship-to-deliver, billing backlog deliver-to-invoice, or treasury drag invoice-to-cash—distinct from shipment-only SLA dashboards or composite perfect-order scoring elsewhere in cat-23.5. Aggregate tail behaviour with ninety-fifth and ninety-ninth percentile splits because enterprise B2B cycles (often eighteen to forty-five elapsed days) exhibit long tails where arithmetic means lie.

Schedule two artefacts: (a) hourly materialized bottleneck panels with ninety-day sliding windows for Planning review; (b) nightly anomaly detection comparing rolling seven-day median pair-hours versus trailing ninety-day baseline per warehouse (`WERKS`) so Operations catches Fulfillment drift before month-end projections freeze.

**Step 3 — Validate against ERP truth**

SAP drill-down: pull five random `order_id` values surfaced above ninety-ninth percentile dwell in pick→pack and reconcile timestamps against VL03N delivery flows and WM confirmations (`LIKP`/`LIPS`) plus SM37 batch IDs when Intermediate Document replay affects postings—ensure Splunk duplicates removed truly correspond to duplicate audit fires, not legitimate rework loops.

Oracle drill-down: compare staging durations against OTBI subject-area extracts or `WSH_DELIVERY_DETAILS.actual_ship_date` versus invoice dates on `RA_CUSTOMER_TRX_ALL`; mismatches trace to timezone normalization (`GMT` versus warehouse local) fixable via props Layer.

NetSuite drill-down: validate Item Fulfillment versus Invoice timestamps inside the UI Transaction timeline for the same internal IDs returned by Splunk—multi-subsidiary rollups occasionally duplicate rows when SuiteAnalytics exposes consolidated currency alongside base currency columns.

Cross-system sanity: reconcile Splunk median invoice-to-cash hours against treasury cash-application extracts (`BSAD` cleared rows against FI postings). Divergence indicates Splunk watermark lag rather than arithmetic error.



Vendor parity checks required before paging: verify against the SAP Fiori Manage dashboard shipment timestamps alongside VL03N line-item postings once plant timezone offsets land in Splunk props; verify against the Oracle OM dashboard workbook tabs tied to `OE_ORDER_HEADERS_ALL` promise clocks before trusting ninety-ninth percentile outliers; verify against the NetSuite dashboard transaction ribbon chips sourced from Lists → Transactions before escalating Supply Chain bridges.

**Step 4 — Operationalize dashboards and accountability**

Dashboard Studio layout suggestion (four bands): upper ribbon single-value tiles for organizational median cycle hours plus subsidiary median Days Sales Outstanding sourced from finance extracts that reuse receivable balances—not Splunk pretending to replace GAAP statements—paired footnotes naming ERP chart-of-accounts ownership.

Middle band waterfall visualization feeding ordered canonical stages (`Ordered→Confirmed→Allocated→Picked→Packed→Shipped→Delivered→Invoiced→Cash_Applied`) using Splunk-supported waterfall viz options or exported aggregates via `xyseries` pivots.

Adjacent band directional sankey diagram summarizing volumetric flows between coarse stages when transitions permit chord sizing—truncate rarely visited hops for readability.

Lower band dual-axis line overlay comparing ninety-day median dwell per warehouse versus enterprise SLA corridor lines Finance publishes quarterly.

Routing guidance: bottleneck deterioration exceeding twenty-five percent week-over-week at pick-pack fires Ops Engineering ticket category **Fulfillment_Systemic_Delay** with Slack bridge #supply-chain-war-room—finance paging reserved for invoice-to-cash regression exceeding treasury-approved Days Sales Outstanding ceiling since credit-release latency differs tactically from dock delays.

Document RACI linking Splunk saved-search IDs to SIOP cadence decks.

**Step 5 — Troubleshooting**

• Flat durations despite visible backlog usually trace duplicate `_time` stamping—inspect props ordering versus ERP clock drift; enforce NTP discipline on middleware emitting JDBC timestamps.

• Parallel Warehouse Management scans posting identical timestamps for pick and pack collapse durations to zero hours though labour elapsed—annotate dashboards when RFID tunnels consolidate timestamps or augment SPL with engineered dwell sourced from Labour Management extracts.

• Month-end SAP backlog loads replay historical CDHDR rows causing artificial spikes—detect `_indextime` minus `_time` skew beyond twelve hours and freeze alerting windows during SAP BW staging refreshes documented by Basis.

• Credit-management holds pause orders between Confirmed→Allocated; resumed flows inflate preceding bucket durations though underlying warehouse remained idle—join Splunk rows optional lookup keyed by credit-block indicator (`VBAK.CMGST`) before interpreting Ops KPI alone.

• Planned ERP maintenance windows suppress transitions altogether—surface Calendar-approved blackout overlays drawn from Change Management CSV lookups.

• EDI ASN latency stretches Delivered timestamps relative to carrier milestones—cross-reference TMS milestones already monitored under sibling shipment-centric UC without duplicating carrier KPI definitions here.

• Partial shipments duplicate lifecycle rows when splitting VBAP quantities—ensure dedup respects smallest granular stage grain per delivery (`delivery_number`) when ERP emits duplicate headers.

• Customer pickup lanes bypass carrier-leg timestamps entirely—segment flows using ship-method lookup flags so executive summaries exclude irrelevant lanes.

• Multi-currency invoicing with FX gain-loss postings near quarter-lock may disturb naive invoice-to-cash math—Finance-supplied FX normalization belongs upstream of Splunk aggregates.

• JDBC pool exhaustion surfaces as duplicated polls—monitor DB Connect internal logs for connection wait spikes before blaming ERP latency.

• Heavy users launching `transaction` spans (`transaction order_id maxspan=180d`) against millions of lifecycle rows exhaust search quotas—prefer streamed bottleneck SPL where feasible; document architectural escape hatch only after Performance Engineering approval.

• Returns-to-stock workflows reopen stages already marked Delivered—maintain auxiliary exclude filters keyed by `return_indicator` columns sourced from reverse-logistics feeds handled under sibling UC-23.5.7 scope.
• JDBC `connection refused` toward the SAP HANA SQL port (`3NN15` pattern) typically signals firewall regression or Basis stopping the indexserver listener—confirm reachability from the DB Connect worker with `telnet` or `nc` before reopening Splunk support tickets.

• Oracle JDBC errors such as `ORA-01017: invalid username/password` after credential rotation indicate the DB Connect Identity still stores the retired secret—update the Identity in the DB Connect UI and rerun Test Connection instead of assuming ERP outage.

• HANA SQLSTATE `42501` / insufficient privilege when selecting packaged views wrapping `VBAK`/`LIKP` means SAP security never granted the integration user SELECT on the CDS expose—Basis must attach the documented role while Splunk administrators avoid widening grants beyond the approved SQL.
• Packaged JDBC extracts occasionally halt with permission denied messages until SAP Basis reapplies the documented analytical role—capture DB Connect stderr alongside warehouse ticket IDs.
grandmaExplanation: We follow each customer order through its business steps—from confirming the order to shipping the goods to getting paid—and measure the hours between those steps so we can see whether delays pile up in the warehouse, on the road, or in billing before cash arrives.
